description: Presbytery standing rules require a January meeting. Minutes not submitted for January 1999.
responses: If this is indeed the case, then it would be very strange as the standing rules are only printed for the January meeting each year and evidently you had these, so you should have had the Winter 1999 Minutes as well. Nevertheless, enclosed is a copy of the January 1999 Minutes with our apology.

description: MNA committee should have been constituted as a commission to serve as a temporary session. BCO 5-3.
responses: They were constituted as a commission, and it was a failure on our part to record this in our minutes. We do apologize and will do better in the future.

description: No record that the candidates were members of local churches or endorsed by sessions. BCO 18-2.
responses: It seems obvious that if we received the candidate that he had to be a member of a church within our presbytery. All the forms were received by the Candidates Committee and all were reported to Presbytery. We shall in the future try to record all information regarding our candidates. Our apology.

description: Presbytery accepted parts of licensure exam for ordination but no record that they asked if their views changed. BCO 21-4.
responses: Of course this was done and we shall try to record all such proper information in the future. Our apology.

description: Church dissolved with no evidence of disposition of property or that members were dealt with. BCO 25-12, 13-10.
responses: This was an inner city church with no real property. We had dealt with the minister previously on a matter of adultery, and he was excommunicated. Membership was almost non-existent by this time. You would have to examine all of our minutes for the last three or four years to understand the whole process and look into the sealed records of
the case. I don't know anymore we could have done to reflect this in our records. We apologize for insufficient data.

description: There is no motion where a TE is approved for transfer from the ARP pending his release. There is no record in his exam that he was asked to ascribe to the questions of ordination. BCO 13-6.
responses: We had received his transfer and thus no motion was needed in this regard, otherwise we would have stated "pending his transfer." All questions were asked and answers given in the affirmative and he then signed the Book of Ministerial Obligations. We did everything in an orderly fashion but if anything was lacking, we apologize and will try to make it clearer in the future.

description: No documentation that a TE has received permission to labor out of bounds of Palmetto Presbytery. BCO 13-2.
responses: The minister in question has permission to labor out of bounds from Palmetto Presbytery. Our responsibility was to examine the man and permit him to labor within our bounds. Both presbyteries did their work according to BCO 13-2. Beyond that we have and need no further documentation. I don't know how much clearer it could be, but we shall try to make it clearer in the future.

description: No record that the candidates were members of local churches or endorsed by sessions. BCO 18-2.
responses: The candidate was a member of a church within Calvary Presbytery and all proper forms were received and reported to Presbytery. We shall try to make it clearer in the future. We apologize.

description: Acceptance of man from another presbytery should have been "pending transfer" from previous presbytery. BCO 13-7.
responses: The transfer had already been received, thus no need for this statement, otherwise we would have so stated. We shall try to make it clearer in the future.

description: Call is not provided, and position called to is not stated, should be received "pending transfer." BCO 20-1, 13-6, 13-7.
responses: The records show that the call and position is stated in the appendix. His transfer was already in hand, thus no need for the statement, "pending his transfer." I don't know how much clearer we can make it. All the information is in the minutes, but we apologize if it is not clear enough.

description: No record of candidates reporting once a year;
responses: These report to the Candidates Committee. Committee members have contact with each of our candidates on a regular basis. A list of candidates and licentiates are included each January in the docket. If these were not carrying out their responsibilities, reporting, etc, they would be removed from the roll. Interns have an individual overseeing

their work and their internships would not be approved without regular reports to the committee and thus to Presbytery. We think it is clear enough, but we shall strive to make it clearer. We do apologize.

description: No record of reports of interns at stated meeting;
responses: See response above.

description: There is no documentation (calls, etc.) for two men for changes from assistant to associate pastor. There are no commissions approved for installation. BCO 15-1, 15-2.
responses: These men were already ordained and installed in their particular churches. This was simply a change in their title designation.
Adopted

description: No record of source of request to dissolve relationship with an Assistant Pastor. BCO 23-1.
No record of Presbytery approving call to TE. BCO 20-1.
TE was installed at the organizational service, but the call was not approved by the congregation until November 8, 1998 and organizational service was held on October 25, 1998. BCO 20-1 & 5-11.
responses: The congregation voted to call TE Jim Powell at a congregational meeting on October 18, 1998 prior to the October 25 organizational meeting, but the moderator did not include the terms of call and did not intend to change them, so the moderator simply neglected to present them. So, while Mr. Powell was installed at the organizational meeting on October 25, there had to be an additional meeting the following Sunday, November 8 to present the congregation with the terms of call, which were then unanimously approved. We respectfully submit this response.
Adopted

description: statement of exceptions byte not included for review as Appendix A as indicated in the minutes.
responses: copy of appropriate page from the minutes of july 20, 1999,
and a copy of the exceptions cited as appendix a enclosed.
A dopted

description: Called meeting and no minutes submitted. BCO 13-11.
responses: Clerk apologizes for omission. Three copies are enclosed.

description: Attachment G. No quorum for commission appointed to install TE. (RE failed to attend). BCO 15-2.
responses: ECP apologizes for not taking the following action at the October 16 meeting: “ECP acknowledges that the commission to install TE Peterson did not have a quorum. By BCO 15-2 and Roberts Rules (RONR), the work they did would be invalid. However, the commission expected the fourth man to arrive by the announced time, 6 PM. Meanwhile, the invited guests had arrived and were anticipating the service. This particular task of a commission is to do work, rather than to deliberate, decide, and execute. The deliberation and decision portion of the installation task was already handled properly by the full ECP. ECP approves the decision, made at 6 PM by the short commission, that the ultimate purpose of the commission would be better served by (a) proceeding in violation of RONR and BCO 15-2 versus (b) announcing the lack of quorum and sending guests home. ECP will strive to appoint more than 4 men to ordain/install commissions in the future.”
Adopted

description: No indication that a congregational meeting was held to approve dissolution of pastoral relationship. BCO 23-1.
Item 19.C & 19.D: Teaching Elders were approved to labor out of bounds without following BCO 8-7 and 13-2.
No record of congregational meetings to dissolve pastoral relationship. BCO 23-1.
Minister examined for transfer with no record of previous ecclesiastical connection. BCO 13-6.
Call for meeting is not in order. BCO 13-12 requires 3RE’s & 3TE’s from 3 churches.
No indication that candidate for li censure preached a sermon as part of his exam. BCO 19-2. d. That the following responses to the 28th GA exceptions be found
Only 11 Session records were examined where all Church Session records are required to be examined (appears that this was only half of the records required) BCO 40-1,2,3.
responses: “ On motion, Great Lakes Presbytery answers General Assembly as follows: ‘Great Lakes Presbytery regrets not documenting
the fulfillment of these two obligations and will be more careful in the
future.’”

description: no mention of candidates reporting or receiving reports from instructors. BCO 18-6.
responses: See response above. A dopted

description: No records of commission reports. BCO 15-1,13-11.
Presbytery entered a judgmental statement against the church without and recording the matter of facts (see dissent 99-77 & protest 99-109) BCO 25-11, 35-3.
responses: Heartland Presbytery apologizes for having “entered a judgmental statement against the church without recording the matter of facts.” In the future we will be more careful to document the facts of the case. The fuller detailing of the facts which should have been recorded are these: (1) Messiah Church was no longer a member of the PCA and therefore no formal process could be brought against it. (Shortly after Heartland Presbytery established a study committee to investigate full preterism, Messiah Church withdrew from the PCA under terms of BCO 25-11.) (2) The presbytery had already approved a motion that declared full preterism to be “heretical” and outside the catholic faith as defined in the ancient creeds. (See “Committee Paper Investigating Full Preterism,” adopted in action 99-71. Note action 99-70 was not concluded until after 99-71 had been voted upon and approved.) (3) Messiah Church had already publicly identified itself as being full preterist in many ways. This public identification was communicated to the presbytery by the witness of two teaching elders and documentation provided by a former ruling elder at Messiah Church. All three affirmed that Messiah Church had sponsored a full preterist conference, had announced it publicly, and one of the advertising bills was presented to presbytery. They also presented testimony that he (the TE) and Messiah Church were working to establish a full preterist denomination, for which he was writing the BCO. They (the witnesses) also affirmed before presbytery that the teaching elder of Messiah Church taught full preterism on radio in the name of the church. (4) Thus, by the standard of Deut. 19:15, we simply took the determination of 99-71 that full preterism was heretical and beyond the pale of the catholic creeds (a determination with which 28th General Assembly found no fault when it acted on our overture) and applied it to the self-declarations of Messiah Church. Thank you for bringing to our attention this oversight of failing to record the facts.
Adopted

description: pi 67, 11.3c, no record of a congregational meeting to request dissolution. TE call not attested or signed. BCO 23-1.
responses: The congregational meeting to request dissolution was done
and reported to Presbytery in 5/98. Presbytery took no action at the time
because the dissolution was subject to disorder, complaints and
irregularities that Presbytery looked into thoroughly prior to the
acceptance of the dissolution. Thus, a good deal of our 1998 minutes
deals with these matters. It was a clerical oversight to neglect to record
the date of dissolution request from the congregation in the minutes
when it was later acted upon. But Presbytery was not rem iss in
addressing the disorders visited upon this congregation and relation with
the pastor. These matters have now, by the grace of God, been brought
to resolution.

description: Complaint against Presbytery referenced in minutes and is not in Appendix HP 39-10 as indicated. BCO 10-4.
responses: As noted in the minutes, the complainant orally withdrew
this complaint on the floor of Presbytery, as the substance of the matter
was resolved. It again was a clerical oversight as this com plaint was not
properly included in the minutes. A dopted

description: Man licensed who “holds exception in his view of the sabbath (he does not believe that the Sabbath Day and Lord’s Day are the same), the law (he is not absolutely sure that the Decalogue is the New Testament Ethic), and on who may administer the Sacraments (he cannot see that the administration of the sacraments is the sole prerogative of teaching elders; he believes that ruling elders and others who have dem onstrated knowledge, understanding, and maturity concerning the sacraments should be allowed to administer them .)” (WCF2X-1, 27-4, 19-2).
responses: New River respectfully resubmits the same response to the
ongoing exception in the case of licentiate terry Roush, as follows:
“Terry Roush has rescinded his exception concerning the Decalogue as
the new testament ethic and expressed a view consistent with the
Reformed Faith. The Presbytery finds his other two exceptions (in the
area of only teaching elders being authorized to administer the
Sacraments and his view that the Sabbath Day and the Lord’s Day are
not the same) as allowable and therefore his license remains in effect.”
in response to the report of the cr pratt he 28th GA, the
Presbytery would note the following items.
as to reason a (“To teach these things at all is to disturb the
peace and purity of the church.”) the presbytery respectfully calls the
attention of the cr pr to the minutes of the 14th General Assembly,
Section 14-52, report of the committee of commissioners on judicial
Business, especially to the final paragraph concerning allowing
exceptions, as follows:
"This allowance of exceptions, however, does not warrant his
teaching or preaching of that matters oas to disturb the peace and
purity of the church. the court of jurisdiction must determine in each
situation whether such unwarranted actions have occurred. ”
(Emphasis added).
New River Presbytery continues to believe we alone have the
right to determine whether the teaching of these exceptions (if such
should occur) should be limited by the Presbytery. We believe that the
cr pr has gone beyond the bounds of their authority in recommending
an exception that would deny us this right without judicial process.
as to reason b (The courts of the church have declined to define the
“fundamentals of the system of doctrine” and they have never limited
them to the results of past judicial decisions.), the Presbytery respectfully
calls the attention of the cr pr to the minutes of the 10th General Assembly, page 103, Section 10-75, report of the committee of
Commissioners on Judicial Business, item 25 (Answer to Q.2 and Q.3,
quoted in part as follows:
"Also, as result of proper judicial processes, judgment may be
made which determinately interpret what may or may not be in accord
with our standards. any other procedure of set tine forth or compiling
a list of essential....doctrines would, in effect, am end the standards by
an unconstitutional method. ” (Emphasis added.)
New River Presbytery continues to believe the ONLY way to
determine that any doctrine is to be viewed throughout the General Assembly as a “fundamental of the system” and therefore requires all
Sessions and Presbyteries to deny license or ordination is through
‘proper judicial process.’ Until such time as these two doctrines have
been upheld by the standing judicial committee of the General Assembly as such ‘fundamentals of the system ’ and Session or
Presbytery is free to grant as they see fit. Again, we believe that the
cr pr has gone beyond the bounds of their authority in recommending
an exception that would declare these two (or any other doctrines) as
“fundamentals of the system” without judicial process.
as to reason c (GA has determined that only te ’s can administer
the Sacraments), New River Presbytery never has allowed anyone but a
TE to administer the sacraments and will not in the future.
Since this is the third year in a row that we have submitted this
same response, New River Presbytery hereby forwards this response
through the Committee on Constitutional Business in accordance with
RAO Section 7-2 as a non-judicial reference, asking that their non
binding advice be forwarded to the cr prof the 29th General Assembly.
No response received.

description: Items 75-17.f., 75-17.g., and 75- 17.i, refer to appendices which are not attached.
responses: A dopted
The following commissioners entered their dissent to the A ssem bly’s
action on Recommendation 32: TE Don Clements, TE William S. Barker, RE
John H Linebarger, TE Jonathan Sedate Albert M oginot, TE David W allover,
RE Virgil Roberts.

description: Candidates are consistently received with no record that the provisions of BCO 18-2 were met.
p.4, #1,3; No evidence of congregational concurrence in dissolution of calls. BCO 23-1.
p.8, #4,5; Two commissions are established with only one ruling elder member.

p.5, A candidate is brought under care when he is not present so he could not answer the question or receive a charge. Also, there is no record of a charge to or prayer for a candidate who is present. BCO 18-3.
p.5, A candidate is approved for ordination with no record in the minutes of a call or of provisions for ordination. BCO 20-1, 21-5.
No evidence that the TE was examined on Sacraments and Church Government. BCO 13-6.
responses: Presbytery regrets the omission of the recording of any of the elements of examination and will endeavor to be more inclusive in the future.

description: AD HOC Committee, A TE cannot by himself ask a Shepherding Committee to visit. It requires request of a session.
responses: Presbytery notes with thanks the requirement for a session invitation in order for the Shepherding Committee to visit a particular church.

description: No evidence that TEs were examined in Theology. BCO 21-4-a-4.
responses: Presbytery regrets the omission of the recording of this element of the examination and will endeavor to be more inclusive in the future.

description: Each area of examination should be indicated. BCO 13-6.
responses: Presbytery regrets the omission of the recording of this element of the examination, and will endeavor to be more inclusive in the future.

description: No indication that candidates were endorsed by their sessions. BCO 18-2.
responses: Presbytery regrets the omission of the recording of this endorsement and will endeavor to be more inclusive in the future.
Adopted

description: There is no reason given or documentation for removal of a candidate from Under Care Roll. BCO 18-7.
Divesting of TE should report 2nd reading. BCO 38-2. Also should assign to church membership. BCO 46-8.
Examination of TE for transfer from another denomination: He was not examined in Bible content, church history or history of the PCA. BCO 13-6, 21-4.
No record that man was a member of a church of NTP for 6 months or that he had the endorsement of his session. BCO 20-1, 13-10.
Twelve month internship requirement reduced to nine months without explanation. BCO 19-7, 19-16.
responses: By common consent, the internship requirement of 12 months for Mark Balthrop was reduced to 9 months because of his previous experience as pastor of a Baptist church for 8 years.
Adopted

description: No minutes were submitted for Northeast Presbytery apparently because the Presbytery divided during 2000. Northern New England Presbytery, which was designated as the continuing body, is reminded to submit the 2000 minutes of northeast presbytery to the 30lh General Assembly,
minutes of called meeting January 23, 1999 are not included. (RAO 14-3).
responses: Presbytery agrees with the Exception. The January 23, 1999
Presbytery Minutes were approved at the March 12 & 13, 1999
Presbytery Meeting but it was too early to send them at that time to the
stated clerk of General Assembly since they were not due to be
reviewed until after the 27th General Assembly meeting in june of 1999
as required by RAO 14-4 (b). The March 12 & 13, 1999 & July 24,
1999 Presbytery Minutes were approved at the October 1 & 2, 1999
presbytery meeting and these minutes we resent to the stated clerk of
General Assembly on October 20, 1999. The January 23, 1999 Minutes
were inadvertently not included with this material. We will be more
careful in the future to submit all the minutes taken during a calendar
year to the stated clerk of General Assembly as required by RAO 14-
4(a) & (b). Corrective Action: Three (3) copies of the january 23, 1999
presbytery minutes are enclosed with this letter to the stated clerk of
General Assembly as required by RAO 14-4(a) for approval by the 29th
General Assembly. A dopted

description: Churches not represented not listed as excused or unexcused. RAO 14-3.c.7. Cited as form in 1999. RAO 14- 6.C.2.
time of meeting not stated. RAO 14-3- c.3. Cited as form in 1999. RAO 14-6.c.2.
No record of the purpose of a called meeting. BCO 13-12, RAO 14-3.c.1
Teaching elder transferred out with no record of the dissolution of the pastoral relationship. BCO 23-1.
A man who failed his licensure examination was approved as ruling elder supply. BCO 19-1, 22-5

No minutes were submitted by Northern New England Presbytery, apparently because they began operation, albeit as the continuing Presbytery, in 2000. Minutes from any meeting in 2000 should have been submitted to the 29th General Assembly and must be submitted to the 30th General Assembly.
No response to 1995 Minutes.
responses: Regarding the Minutes for May 5, 2000, inasmuch as no response was offered to an exception taken to minutes of May, 1995, in which a Commission is established by the Presbytery to discipline a church member without describing the reasons (BCO 11-4, 31-1, 13-9), the Pacific Presbytery offers its apology to the General Assembly for the oversight in our minutes of May 20, 1995, and for our failure to respond to the General Assembly's exception. Although it is difficult at present to reconstruct the reasons for which a Commission was formed to discipline a church member, we do commit ourselves to give greater diligence in the future both to the proper form of our minutes and to timely responses to the General Assembly.

description: 1997 Minutes: Although 1997 minutes were submitted, the minutes did not contain a copy of the Directory or Standing Rules.
The Moderator appointed a commission without action of the Presbytery.
responses: Presbytery acknowledges the error and has acted to avoid similar lapses by amendment of its Standing Rules.
Adopted

description: Two Teaching Elders granted permission to labor out of ecclesiastical bounds without recording that the requirements of BCO 8-7 and 20-1 were met.
installed a man who is not a pastor of a particular church.
responses: Rocky Mountain Presbytery agrees with the exception and
promises to be more careful in the future. (It does not seem feasible or
appropriate to attempt to correct this action, a sit is now a matter of
history. BCO 8-7 describes the provision for a Presbytery approving the
call of a teaching elder to work wi than organization outside the
jurisdiction of the PCA. it has been the practice of this presbytery to
provide an installation service for our teaching elders called to such other
ministries, if they desire and request such a service. by action of the
Presbytery as its Winter stated meeting on January 26, 2001, by a motion
duly made, seconded, debated and carried, adopted the policy of
conducting commissioning services for men who desire and request such
after the presbytery has approved a call to a teaching elder to work with
an organization outside the jurisdiction of the PCA.

description: questions of a minister were asked, but not the questions of BCO. (BCO 21-9)
responses: Rocky Mountain Presbytery agrees with the exception and
promises to be more careful in the future. (It does not seem feasible or
appropriate to attempt to correct this action, a sit is now a matter of
history and occurred during an optional installation service referred to in
the exception addressed above.)
A dopted

description: paragraph 31.10, concerning the ordination examination of a man. BCO 21-4. there is no indication of completion of internship. there is no indication of approval of educational requirements and original languages. There was no sermon approved and no documentation of his call. ashe was coming as a Licentiate, there was no indication of his being asked if he changed his previous views concerning the WCF, WSC, or the BCO. BCO 21-4.
Theological paper approved in which candidate stated “I believe that God gives extraordinary gifts (miracles, healing, and tongues)”. WCF 18-3; WLC #80; BCO 7-1.
responses: Presbytery respectfully disagrees with the exception. The candidate was thoroughly examined by our Credentials Committee and on the floor of Presbytery. Both the Committee and Presbytery concluded that the views of the candidate were in complete conformity with the Constitution of the PCA. Presbytery is puzzled by the citations that were listed as justification for the exception: WCF 18 is of Assurance of Grace and Salvation and section 3 elaborates on assurance. WLC #80 asks, “Can true believers be infallibly assured that they are in the estate of grace, and that they shall persevere therein unto salvation.” BCO 7-1 ties extraordinary gifts to new revelation. No where in his Theological paper did the candidate make any mention of new or continuing revelation or challenge or question the PCA position on assurance of salvation. The exception should read in the full context in its section of “Views on Charismatic Movement.”
While Presbytery is correct with regard to the second sentence of each exception (“no extraordinary clause stated as rational”), the response to

the first sentence of each (“BCO 13-6 requires thorough exam of transfers in all areas”) is not acceptable. When a man is received from outside the PCA BCO 13-6 requires more elements to the examination than are listed for these men. BCO 13-6 specifies that for men from other denominations all areas of examinations specified in BCO 21-4 were (and are) required.

description: BCO 13-6 requires thorough exam of transfers in all areas. No extraordinary clause stated as rationale.
responses: Presbytery respectfully disagrees with both exceptions. The men transferring were given a knowledge examination and were asked the questions put to candidates at their ordination which they answered in the affirmative (BCO 13-6). The examination followed the requirements of D-21, Check List For Reception of a Minister From Another Denomination (copy enclosed). This Check List is from the “Handbook For Presbytery Clerks – July 1999” prepared by the Office of the GA Stated Clerk. On page one of examiner David M. Smith’s worksheet in answer to Question he wrote, “Only partial – not complete” in answer to Question he wrote, Only partial – not complete.”

description: BCO 13-6 requires thorough exam of transfers in all areas. No extraordinary clause found.
responses: See above response.
Adopted

description: The purpose of the called meeting is not stated with the minutes. BCO 13-12.
This is an exam for ordination not transfer. BCO 13-6 does not apply. Candidate should have been examined in Church History and English Bible.
responses: Adopted

description: Presbytery granted a TE permission to continue laboring out of bounds as a pastor of an independent reformed church in Michigan. 1) The record doesn't include a call or Presbytery's approval of a call, 2) There is no assurance that the TE will be allowed to teach and preach the whole council of God's word, and 3) There is no record that the TE had contacted Great Lakes Presbytery for approval to work within the bounds of that Presbytery. BCO 20-11 & 21-1.
responses: In January of 1999 the Tennessee Valley Presbytery (TVP) improperly granted TE Steve Simmons permission to labor out of bound of the TVP because of his call to serve a non-PCA congregation in Michigan.

The TVP should have corresponded with the Great Lakes Presbytery concerning TE Simmons' pending move. Furthermore, they should have encouraged TE Simmons to transfer his credentials to the Great Lakes Presbytery that he might receive their permission to labor out of bounds within the Presbytery.
The TVP regrets its error in this matter.
In the Lord's gracious providence, TE Simmons has, as of October 2000, been received as a member of the Great Lakes Presbytery, thus correcting the TVP's error.
Thank you for calling our attention to this error.
Adopted

description: Presbytery divested TE Wayne Fair without censure, but the record does not reflect his request for divestiture from the previous meeting.
responses: Mr. Fair requested divestiture in writing at the October, 1998 Stated Meeting. Due to error on the part of the clerk, the subsequent vote was not docketed until the July, 1999 Stated Meeting. My apologies to the committee and the General Assembly for this oversight and for my being out of compliance with the BCO.

description: No minutes submitted for 1997 (RAO 14-4.a&b).
responses: Due to error on the part of the clerk, the 1997 Minutes were not received from the printer in time to be submitted by the deadline. The 1997 minutes are available and will be submitted to the committee if requested. My apologies to the committee and the General Assembly for my violation of the RAO.
Adopted

description: The exceptional clause was invoked for a candidate but Presbytery failed to specify the following: a) did not specify which part of the examination was omitted, b) “Abnormal educational experience” is not sufficiently clear to warrant a waiver, and c) no record of % vote to invoke the exceptional clause.
responses: we acknowledge the lack of record of the examination. A
review of the notes does not reflect the particulars of the examination.
We will make every effort to keep more accurate records than this
incident reflects. Adopted
